text:
<@U0378MDUG1Y> You're right about the origins of the term _open source_, but I would disagree with language/framing in that article.

Admittedly, I was young and naive at the time, but my recollection is that "open source" just felt like a slightly more pragmatic way of pushing the same revolutionary ideas. From today's perspective, it's easy to underestimate, but I think the whole idea did seem like a big threat to the established software companies back then.

elements.text: You're right about the origins of the term
open source

, but I would disagree with language/framing in that article.

Admittedly, I was young and naive at the time, but my recollection is that "open source" just felt like a slightly more pragmatic way of pushing the same revolutionary ideas. From today's perspective, it's easy to underestimate, but I think the whole idea did seem like a big threat to the established software companies back then.

text: For a while now, I've been wishing someone would write a book about this — how FOSS went from being a threat to the established software corps, to be co-opted and ultimately a source of their power.

elements.text: For a while now, I've been wishing someone would write a book about this — how FOSS went from being a threat to the established software corps, to be co-opted and ultimately a source of their power.

text:
<@U01DWJSAF2B> My memories agree with yours. The discussion was about "pragmatic" Open Source vs. "fundamentalist" Free Software. In scientific research, where software was at that time absolutely not recognized as a valuable contribution, we all (i.e. the small minority of youngsters believing in software) jumped on "Open Source" in order to be more relevant, hoping to get from industrial users the recognition that we couldn't get from academia.

Of course, most of the issues the article addresses didn't exist back then. No entity dominated anything in tech space (as far as research was concerned, maybe IBM already dominated banking etc.).

elements.text:
My memories agree with yours. The discussion was about "pragmatic" Open Source vs. "fundamentalist" Free Software. In scientific research, where software was at that time absolutely not recognized as a valuable contribution, we all (i.e. the small minority of youngsters believing in software) jumped on "Open Source" in order to be more relevant, hoping to get from industrial users the recognition that we couldn't get from academia.

Of course, most of the issues the article addresses didn't exist back then. No entity dominated anything in tech space (as far as research was concerned, maybe IBM already dominated banking etc.).

text: aside: The prevailing culture was *very* different from today. *All* source was closed and proprietary. The first tutorial in every university computer course began with a lecture about our allotted budget for doing computer homework. I remember being allowed to use $20.00 for doing assignments in one course. I ended up using only $10.00 and used the other $10.00 to "pay" for printing out all of the source code to Utah Lisp on fan-fold paper.

text:
There's this great <https://www.jstor.org/stable/3317870?origin=crossref|paper> on how the PC revolution became co-opted and it describes a couple of different strategies for how it can happen.

> In a _regularization_ strategy people try to fix social reality in the terms laid down by the dominant ideology, controlling social situations so that the ideal principles of social life become real and palpable.
> _Adjustment_ strategies seek to redress this stigma, not by rejecting the dominant value system, but by attempting to portray the marginal personas a valuable human being within the dominant value framework.
> _Reconstitution_ strategies stem from a conscious attempt to rewrite the rules, change the dominant ideology, and reshape the social context. Such strategies frequently make use of symbolic inversions or reversal.
I've been thinking about how such a framework applies to FOSS:
• the free software movement used a _reconstitution_ strategy aiming to challenge the dominant ideology of closed/proprietary software with inversions that source code should be freely available, modifiable, and unable to become proprietary (copyleft). It's worth noting though that free software was a reactionary movement, aiming to recreate the golden era of software of the 60s/70s, where all software was in the public domain. I think such nostalgia failed to account for the starkly different environment that was, particularly with the advent of microprocessors and digital copyright.
• The open source movement looked at the _reconstitution_ strategy of free software as a failure because businesses (run by business people) misunderstood what "free software" meant. I totally agree with you that the early OS advocates wanted to carry forward the ideals of the free software movement. But they did so with an _adjustment_ strategy, figuring out how to market free software within the dominant system of business and proprietary software. Adjustment strategies carry an ambivalence towards the dominant ideology, and I think for open source that (eventually) meant departing the "ideological" copyleft licensing of free software towards more permissible licensing that proprietary software can use. Thus marking the beginning of how open source became enclosed and extracted.
